text: placed around the perimeter, as if I’m in the waiting room of some shady doctor, sketchy lawyer, or discount dentist. Plastic chairs, and glass tables with brass lamps, magazines scattered around—it’s as if the entire space was purchased out of a sitcom or some mundane reality show. There is nothing special about this room, the sliding glass windows on the far side hiding whatever secretary or assistant might lurk there. I decided to just show up in the middle of the day, thinking that might be the most logical time to catch them open. There is nobody else here but me, so I walk to the window and rap on the pane of glass. A young woman slides it open, chewing her gum as if there is no tomorrow. She takes a long look at me as I open my mouth to speak, and then holds up her finger to silence me. “Don’t tell me—you met a man, he told you to stop by, and here you are, right?” I nod my head. “Yeah, you reek of it, have a seat, don’t need your name, in fact, please don’t tell it to me, the less I know of you, the better. Go sit ALL the way over there in the corner, and I’ll tell Jerry you’re here.” I start to talk and she waves her manicured nails at me, pulling a pencil out of her prodigious hairdo, squinting her eyes, as she closes the window. She wants nothing to do with me. And I don’t blame her. When I turn around there are three people seated in the room with me, and I take a step back, jostled by their presence. To the left of me is a bedraggled man who is either the second coming of Christ or homeless. His long hair and beard are dotted with motes and flies, a scribbled impurity surrounding him like a cloud of dust. There is a wave of heat coming off him like an oven, and the stench that floats to me is a combination of sour milk, rotting meat, and fermented apples. I gag and cover my mouth. He lowers his gaze from me, picking at the sores on his arms, torn flesh that doesn’t want to heal. “Sorry,” I mutter, and he gently shakes his head. This is when I notice the bandages. On his hands, there are so many dirty white bandages that I’m not sure where his hands and fingers begin, and where they end. He seems to have been in a terrible accident, but the more I take him in, the more I realize it’s something else entirely. He is missing fingers, or parts of fingers, digits, or individual segments. Though he is dressed in ratty jeans, boots, and a dingy blue sweater, I see seeping wounds, divots of flesh, missing parts from head to toe. I turn to head to my seat and hear a gentle cough from my right side. I see a young woman, stylish and slim, dressed as though this was a job interview at a top modeling agency downtown, or perhaps an internship at some prestigious fashion magazine. She is quite literally the opposite of whatever the man is—smiling, pretty, clean, and with a shimmer in her gaze. It’s only when I stare directly at her that I realize that she is translucent. When she moves her hand to fix her hair, which is totally fine, I can see through her flesh, to her veins, and the blood running through them, to the bones, and their movement, one joint connected and moving another piece. When she laughs, averting her eyes, fidgeting in her seat now, she fades a bit more, and sensing this shift, coughs, and covers her mouth. Her smile is gone now, and in her eyes I see fear. “Do you…” I start and she shakes her head. “No, it’s okay, don’t…” she says, and fades a bit more. “But, can I…” “Please,” she says, smiling weakly. “Maybe there is…” And she continues to fade, her smile turns to a frown, and as she opens her mouth she disappears. Gone, in a whisper of smoke. I turn to the man on my left, looking for answers. “She said please,” he growls. “How about you shut up and sit down?” I nod my head, and head to the back. In the other corner is a large Black man. He is one of the biggest men I have ever seen. He is wearing only a black tank top and matching shorts, his feet bare. I wonder if he is a professional wrestler, or perhaps a football player, or bouncer. But his size is not what pulls me in, it’s the tattoos that cover his skin. I’ve never seen white tattoos on a Black man, and they cover him in patterns, symbols, glyphs, hieroglyphics, and words. I am captivated by the tapestry that is his skin, and take a step towards him, to try to take it in. He holds up his hand and motions for me to stop. “This is not for you,” he says, in a deep, rumbling voice. He sneezes, and reaches for a box of tissues, but the red mist that has left his nose is already spraying the space in front of him. He dots his nose, and staunches the bleeding, swallowing as his eyes shift to yellow. I take a step back, already out of the reach of his fluids, but fearful nonetheless. “Sorry,” I say. He nods his head, closes his eyes, and I sneak a glance at his formidable arm, a Bible quote running up and down his meaty limb—”…they shall fall by the sword: their infants shall be dashed in pieces, and their women with child shall be ripped up…” and I step away, unwilling to finish the quote, as his eyes snap open. “I said this isn’t for you,” he bellows, as dots of blood cry down his face.
